Why Special Needs Planning Is Important

Special needs planning is important because it allows you to provide for your loved ones as they grow. It also allows you to protect your loved one from becoming a public charge. If your loved one receives public benefits, they may lose those benefits if they receive gifts or inheritances. Special needs planning allows you to provide for your loved one while protecting their benefits.

Special needs planning can help you coordinate care across multiple providers and agencies in the Fort Worth area. When you work with a team that understands both the legal and practical sides of these plans, you can streamline support for your loved one and avoid the difficulties that come with managing care alone. In Tarrant County, local resources often align with well-structured plans, creating seamless support for families.

It is also important to plan for the future because your loved one's needs will change over time. As they grow, their needs will change, and you will need to adjust your plan to meet their changing needs.

Additionally, planning ahead protects against sudden changes in your financial situation or health, which could otherwise create major challenges. By setting up these plans now, you give loved ones a smoother transition during changes and bring clarity to those who will help care for them.

Special needs planning is not just about securing resources, but also about empowering individuals with disabilities to lead fulfilling lives. Clearly defined goals and support structures help families navigate challenges with confidence and turn their focus to supporting growth and independence.
Benefits of Special Needs Planning

Special needs planning provides numerous benefits for you and your loved one. It allows you to provide for your loved ones as they grow. It also allows you to protect your loved one from becoming a public charge. Finally, special needs planning allows you to create a plan to meet your loved one's changing needs as they grow.

Frequently Asked Questions
What Is a Special Needs Trust?

A special needs trust is a legal arrangement that lets a person with a physical or mental disability, or a chronic illness, receive income without threatening eligibility for public disability benefits like Social Security, Supplemental Security Income, Medicare, or Medicaid. In the Dallas-Fort Worth area, these trusts help families secure financial futures for loved ones without losing access to these crucial benefits.

A trustee manages the trust, overseeing how funds are distributed according to strict legal requirements. This setup ensures your loved one receives thoughtful, sustained care that covers healthcare, housing, and other essential needs.
Who Should Serve as a Trustee?

Trustee selection is a key aspect of managing a special needs trust. The right trustee will grasp your beneficiary's needs and your family’s goals, have solid financial management skills, and understand legal standards. A trustee may be a family member, close friend, or a professional organization such as a bank or trust company.

When choosing a trustee, consider their reliability with finances, availability, and willingness to learn the beneficiary’s needs. Many families opt for professional trustees to help manage the complexities, keeping the trust compliant and helping it benefit the individual for years.
What Happens If My Loved One Needs to Move Out of State?

If your loved one with a special needs trust set up in Fort Worth needs to move to another state, several factors will come into play. Laws about special needs trusts and eligibility for public benefits differ by state, which can affect trust management.

Review state rules and be ready to adjust trust provisions if needed. At Crain & Wooley, we guide families through these transitions, making sure your trust continues to serve its purpose wherever life takes your loved one. Our attorneys help you understand changes and make necessary adjustments so that your planning stays strong and effective.
What Steps Are Involved in Setting Up a Special Needs Trust?

Establishing a special needs trust involves several steps, starting with a consultation to determine which trust type suits your family. Consider your beneficiary’s needs and financial situation before drafting the legal documents, which will outline trust terms, beneficiaries, and trustees. After the trust is created, you must fund it with assets such as property, inheritances, or other resources. Compliance with federal and state laws is critical throughout this process.
Crain & Wooley assists Fort Worth families at every stage, giving clear advice and making sure every step fits your broader planning goals and meets all regulations.
Can Public Benefits Be Affected by a Special Needs Trust?

Improperly distributing assets can threaten eligibility for government benefits. When created and managed properly, a special needs trust will not jeopardize Supplemental Security Income (SSI) and Medicaid eligibility. Families in the Fort Worth area should work with attorneys who can ensure the trust complements—not interferes with—public benefits.

Funds should pay for non-support expenses like education, medical care, home modifications, or equipment to protect benefit eligibility. At Crain & Wooley, we help families align their trusts with benefit rules while supporting loved ones.

Assisting Clients With Tax Planning in Texas

At Crain & Wooley, we help clients of all backgrounds plan for costly estate taxes on the assets they plan to pass on to loved ones and other beneficiaries. Our dedicated legal team in Dallas-Fort Worth is familiar with the various components of gift taxes, estate taxes, and generation-skipping transfer taxes, and we have the extensive resources you need to minimize or avoid tax liability.
Call Crain & Wooley today at (972) 945-1610 or contact us online to schedule a consultation with our tax planning attorney in Dallas-Fort Worth.
What is Tax Planning?

Tax planning is considered the organization of finances to minimize tax liability while complying with tax laws and regulations. It often involves analyzing your financial situation, identifying possible tax-saving opportunities, and implementing strategies to optimize your tax position. Effective tax planning helps individuals and businesses maximize their after-tax income, preserve wealth, and achieve financial goals.
Tax Planning Matters We Handle
Call or visit our law office today to discuss any of the following tax planning strategies:

items: Charitable Giving: You can take income tax deductions for money or assets you donate to charity. You can also use charitable remainder trusts, charitable lead trusts, or family foundations to avoid capital gains and estate taxes.

Assets in Trust: There are different types of trusts that can be used to protect assets and reduce the size of your taxable estate. We can help you determine if your estate plan should include grantor retained annuity trusts (GRATs), irrevocable life insurance trusts (ILITs), education trusts, or special needs trusts.

Annual Gifting: You can transfer money or assets to your family members and other loved ones to reduce the total value of your estate. This tax strategy lowers the amount of estate taxes that will be due after you pass away. Annual gifting allows your assets to be transferred to beneficiaries without incurring costly gift and estate taxes.

Helping Clients Obtain Peace of Mind

People often think that trusts are only for the rich and famous trying to hide hundreds of millions of dollars in offshore bank accounts. However, normal, everyday people can greatly benefit from using guardianship trusts.

Trusts, by far, are the most comprehensive of all estate planning options because they greatly simplify estate settlement. Trusts can also help your family avoid the hassle of probate and legal disputes over assets and properties you want your beneficiaries to inherit.
Protect your legacy today—Contact a Dallas-Fort Worth trust attorney at Crain & Wooley. Call (972) 945-1610.
What is a Trust?

A trust is a legal arrangement in which one party, known as the "settlor" or "grantor," transfers ownership of assets to another party, known as the "trustee," for the benefit of a third party, known as the "beneficiary." Trusts are commonly used for various purposes, including estate planning, asset protection, and charitable giving.

They offer flexibility and control over asset distribution and can be customized for specific needs—whether safeguarding family property, supporting a cause, or planning for long-term care. By allowing the grantor to set detailed instructions, trusts ensure assets are handled exactly as intended.
Common Types of Trusts

There are a variety of trusts that you can include in your estate plan that can be tailored to meet your unique planning needs. Our DFW trust lawyers are here to discuss your goals and determine which type of trust meets your specific goals.
We have years of experience establishing and administering the following types of trusts:

items: Special Needs Trust
Revocable Living Trust
Irrevocable Trust
Minor Trusts
Descendant’s Trusts
Educational Trust
Taxable Estate Trusts and more

bodyBlocks.text: We also provide guidance with Trust Administration and Trust Litigation when disputes arise or documents require judicial interpretation.

In addition to these, trust options also include Spendthrift Trusts, which protect beneficiaries from their potentially detrimental spending habits, and Grantor Retained Annuity Trusts (GRATs), which are designed to minimize gift taxes when transferring assets. Understanding your unique situation is pivotal, and our knowledgeable team is well-equipped to help you navigate these options.
Protect Your Assets with a Trust in Fort Worth

Trusts are adaptable legal instruments that can be tailored to your specific needs. Whether it's a revocable living trust, an irrevocable trust, or a charitable trust, we can help. Our firm's skilled DFW trust attorneys can guide you through the complexity of selecting the best trust for your case.
There are several common types of trusts that can be tailored to your specific needs, including:

items: Revocable Living Trust: Allows you to maintain control of your assets during your lifetime and avoid probate.
Irrevocable Trust: Provides asset protection and potential tax benefits, but cannot be modified or revoked.
Charitable Trust: Allows you to support your favorite charitable causes while potentially reducing your tax liability.

bodyBlocks.text: By including a trust in your estate plan, you can enjoy a range of benefits, such as:

items: Asset protection
Probate avoidance
Privacy
Control over asset distribution
Potential tax savings

bodyBlocks.text: Our knowledgeable trust attorneys in Dallas-Fort Worth can guide you through the process of choosing the right trust for your unique circumstances. Contact us to schedule a consultation and start protecting your assets for the future.
What Are the Benefits of Including a Trust in Your Estate Plan?
Some of the benefits gained from establishing a trust include:

items: Avoiding In-State and Out-of-State Probate : Unlike a traditional last will and testament, trusts are portable. For example, if you have real estate in multiple states or other countries, then a trust would be worth considering because your beneficiaries would avoid the probate process in each state or country in which property is owned. Click here to find out why a will is not enough if you want to avoid the hassle of probate.

Plan for Nursing Home Expenses: As we age, we need to be prepared for the possibility of living in a nursing home. With the average monthly costs of nursing home care in Texas being $6,000 or more, an irrevocable trust protects your assets from being spent on nursing home costs. The greatest benefit is received by having a nursing home plan in place FIVE YEARS before the need for long-term care.

Protecting Loved Ones with Special Needs: Leaving an intentional legacy for a loved one with special needs can be accomplished through creating a special needs trust. With a special needs trust, you won't have to worry about your loved one losing any type of public assistance already being received. Read our blog to find out how special needs trusts can be powerful estate planning tools for disabled beneficiaries.

Protecting Assets from Troubled Heirs: Life is hard, and sometimes, loved ones struggle with mental health issues or harmful behaviors like substance abuse. Creating a trust with special instructions offers protection for both your assets and loved ones who tend to take financial risks. With a revocable living trust, you can restrict how and when money is given to certain beneficiaries. However, some complicated provisions can backfire, which is why you should consult with a lawyer to discuss your concerns.

Protecting a Surviving Spouse: Did you know that a surviving spouse does not always inherit from their deceased spouse’s estate? Surviving spouses are often surprised to learn that they must complete the probate process if they want to obtain 100% ownership of estate assets. However, a trust provides a seamless and court-free way for a surviving spouse to carry on living and making financial decisions. A trust also provides asset protection should the surviving spouse remarry.

Providing for Minor Children or Grandchildren: Trusts can also provide an avenue for safeguarding a minor’s inheritance until a suitable level of maturity or life experience is gained. Trusts can also be used to make sure younger beneficiaries receive funding for college or vocational training. If you are a Texan who owns mineral rights that have been passed down from your ancestors, then you can put the rights into a revocable living trust to ensure they pass on to future generations.

Addressing Blended Family Concerns: Family is sometimes complicated. Trusts offer the ability to ensure equitable treatment of children and other family members in multiple marriage situations. Texas is a community property state. Misunderstanding Texas community property laws can cause serious problems for blended families. For example, the property doesn’t automatically go to the surviving spouse, which means children from a previous marriage can end up inheriting the decedent’s half of the property to the detriment of the surviving spouse.

Avoiding Estate Taxes: Taxes play an important part in civil discourse, but creating a trust may prohibit the government from taking more than its fair share. With a revocable living trust, your beneficiaries will be able to avoid the probate process and negative tax implications. Click here to find out about the tax benefits of including a revocable living trust in your estate plan.

Supporting Charities: Leaving a legacy of generosity is made easy through the creation of a trust. Our lawyers can include instructions in the trust for referencing a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU), which will ensure your hard-earned assets are utilized with the particular charitable goals you have in mind.

Can a Trust Be Changed?
Yes, a trust can be changed under certain conditions, and understanding these conditions is crucial for effective estate planning.
Here are the main factors that determine whether a trust can be changed:

items: Revocable trusts are flexible: The grantor can modify or revoke the trust at any time during their lifetime. This allows updates to beneficiaries, trustees, and specific instructions as family or financial needs change.

Irrevocable trusts are generally permanent: Changes can only be made under limited circumstances, such as approval from all beneficiaries or a court order under Texas law. These trusts are often used for asset protection and tax planning.
Legal guidance is key: Texas trust law can be complex, so consulting with a trust attorney Fort Worth residents trust is essential before making any amendments.

bodyBlocks.text: As the name implies, revocable trusts allow the grantor to make changes or withdraw the trust entirely at any time while they are still alive. Revocable trusts are a popular choice because of their adaptability, which lets beneficiaries, trustees, and rules be changed as needed. These modifications are frequently required when the grantor's objectives for estate planning, family relationships, or finances significantly alter.

Conversely, irrevocable trusts are usually intended to be irreversible after they are created. This rigidity is deliberate since irrevocable trusts are frequently used for beneficially permanent reasons, such as asset protection, tax planning, and charitable giving. It is feasible to make changes to an irrevocable trust, nevertheless, under specific situations. These usually need approval from all beneficiaries and, depending on the details of the trust and the relevant state legislation, may entail a non-judicial settlement agreement or court approval.

Seeking advice from Crain & Wooley, a Dallas-Fort Worth trust law firm will help you understand whether you can change your trust and how to go about making any necessary adjustments. Our knowledgeable lawyers can help you navigate the complexity of trust law so that your estate plan continues to reflect your changing objectives and needs. Professional legal counsel is essential whether you need to amend an existing trust or are thinking about establishing one.
Can a Trustee Remove a Beneficiary From a Trust in TX?
There are important considerations if you want to know whether a trustee can remove a beneficiary in Texas:

items: Grantor usually holds the power: In most cases, only the grantor (the person who set up the trust) can add or remove beneficiaries, and not the trustee.

Trustee's role is limited: A trustee's duty is to manage the trust according to its terms. Unless the trust document specifically grants this power (which is rare), the trustee cannot alter beneficiaries.

Probate court involvement: When the grantor passes away and the trust becomes irrevocable, changes to beneficiaries may require going to probate court and filing a petition, especially in the absence of clear instructions in the trust.

bodyBlocks.text: In most trusts, trustees do not have the power to change the beneficiary of an established trust. The right to add and remove beneficiaries is a power reserved for the grantor of the trust; however, the grantor of the trust can name themselves as a trustee for the duration of their lifetime with a revocable living trust and make changes. When the grantor dies, their trust will usually become irrevocable and cannot be modified without going to probate court and filing a petition.
Can a Trustee Be a Beneficiary of an Irrevocable Trust in TX?
If you are considering naming a trustee who is also a beneficiary in Texas, keep these key facts in mind:

items: It is allowed: Texas law permits a trustee to also be a beneficiary, and this is common in family trusts.

Potential conflict of interest: The trustee still has a fiduciary duty to act in the best interest of all beneficiaries, so appointing a trustee/beneficiary requires careful drafting of the trust document to avoid disputes.
Legal guidance is recommended: Consulting a trust lawyer Fort Worth families count on ensures the trust complies with state law and any potential conflicts are managed proactively.

bodyBlocks.text: Yes. An irrevocable trust does not allow modifications, so the grantor made a permanent transfer of assets and it is not uncommon to name a spouse, child or other family member to be named a trustee and beneficiary.
A trustor should be cautious when choosing a beneficiary as their trustee due to the conflict of interest.

Understanding Wills: Basics and Benefits
What Is a Will?

A last will and testament is probably the most common form of estate planning. It is typically a typewritten document that is signed in front of a Notary Public. Your will states your wishes regarding the disbursement of assets and allows you to:

items: Designate who receives your assets
Designate who will oversee your estate (Executor)
Provide for certain life scenarios, like a child preceding you in death
Ensure the court has a record of your last wishes

bodyBlocks.text: While a will establishes how assets are distributed, it typically must go through the probate process in Texas. Probate ensures the court recognizes the will and authorizes the Executor to carry out your instructions. In the Dallas–Fort Worth area, probate can take several months depending on the complexity of the estate.
For many families, a will works best when coordinated with other tools such as a revocable living trust or financial planning strategies. We help you evaluate which structure best fits your needs.
What Happens If I Pass Away Without A Will?

If you pass away without a valid will, you risk your estate being contested by other parties. If this occurs, your intended beneficiaries will have to go to court to fight for the assets and property that they are entitled to. The best way to avoid unintended consequences with important estate matters is to hire an experienced lawyer to draft your last will and testament.
When Should I Write My Will?
Creating a will should be proactive, not reactive. You should get started on writing your will if you are married, have kids, or have a positive net worth.

Starting a will is often best tackled earlier than most people realize. Life changes rapidly, and ensuring your will reflects your current circumstances can protect assets and dictate their distribution accurately. For young adults, especially those embarking on significant life changes such as buying a home, starting a family, or launching a business, a will ensures that your assets and personal wishes are respected and followed during unforeseen events.
Steps to Create a Will: A Clear Guide
How to Create a Will?

items: Determine the Appropriate Will Type: Identify the most suitable type of will for your circumstances.
Outline Your Assets: Compile a list of assets you wish to include in your will, ensuring thoroughness and clarity.
Designate Beneficiaries: Thoughtfully decide who will inherit your assets, considering their needs and your wishes.
Select an Executor: Choose a trusted individual to carry out the instructions in your will efficiently and responsibly.
Address Guardianship for Minors: If you have underage children, carefully designate a guardian who will provide them with care and support.
Consider Charitable Contributions: While optional, you may opt to include a charitable organization as a beneficiary in your will.
Witness and Sign: Finalize your will by signing it in the presence of witnesses, ensuring legal validity.
Secure Storage: Store your will in a secure location, such as a safe or with a trusted advisor, to prevent loss or tampering.
Regular Review and Updates: Periodically revisit your will, making necessary adjustments to reflect significant life changes or updates in your preferences.

bodyBlocks.text: Creating a legally sound will involves thorough preparation and understanding of the legalities involved. Each step in drafting a will ensures that all necessary details regarding your assets and last wishes are meticulously documented. For instance, beneficiaries should be informed of their status in your will to avoid surprises that could lead to conflicts. Additionally, reviewing your will with the assistance of a lawyer, especially after significant life events, helps in making necessary amendments reflecting your current wishes.

Writing a will is an essential step in ensuring that your assets are distributed according to your wishes after you pass away. items: Peace of mind knowing your loved ones are taken care of
Clear instructions for asset distribution
Minimization of potential conflicts among family members
Protection of your children's future
Avoidance of intestacy laws determining asset distribution

It can also reduce the burden on your loved ones during an emotional time by providing clarity and legal direction. Furthermore, through a will, you can appoint trusted individuals to manage your estate, ensuring your legacy is protected, and family dynamics are preserved. Creating a Valid Texas Will: Key Requirements
What Are The Requirements For Creating a Valid Texas Will?

Creating a valid will in the state of Texas is crucial for ensuring that your wishes are honored and your assets are distributed according to your intentions. As experienced Dallas-Fort Worth will lawyers, we can guide you through the process to ensure that your will meets all legal requirements.
Below are some of the key requirements for ensuring that your will is valid in Texas:

items: Age and Capacity: The individual making the will, known as the testator, must be at least 18 years old. The testator must also possess testamentary capacity, which entails understanding the nature of creating a will and its implications.
Written Document: A written will is required. Texas does not accept oral wills; thus, having a written will is crucial.
Proper Signature: The testator must sign the will with two witnesses present. The will must also be signed by the witnesses.

Witnesses: The will must be witnessed by at least two credible witnesses who are at least 14 years old. These witnesses must sign the will in the presence of the testator. Ideally, the witnesses should not be beneficiaries to avoid potential conflicts of interest.

Self-Proving Affidavit: A self-proving affidavit is not necessary, but it can make the probate process easier. In order to verify the legality of the will and do away with the requirement for witnesses to testify in court, this entails the testator and witnesses signing an affidavit in front of a notary public.

bodyBlocks.text: Ensuring the validity of your will is a fundamental step in safeguarding your estate. By understanding and adhering to state-specific legal guidelines, you minimize the risk of disputes or invalidations. Engaging a knowledgeable attorney to review the legal nuances further ensures the smooth administration of your estate, offering your beneficiaries a streamlined probate process. Your attorney can also provide valuable insights on maximizing estate tax benefits and asset protection through proper will structuring, ensuring your estate is managed according to your exact wishes.

Many families also benefit from choosing a firm that can stay with them over time as circumstances change. Working with a team that offers clear education, periodic reviews, and convenient in-person or virtual meetings allows you to keep your plan current without feeling overwhelmed. This kind of long-term relationship can make it easier for your executor and beneficiaries to work with the same trusted professionals when it is time to carry out the instructions in your will.
The Role of a Will Lawyer in Fort Worth

Choosing a will lawyer near you in Fort Worth brings numerous advantages in terms of accessibility and personalized service. A local will lawyer is not only familiar with Texas state laws but also understands the nuances of regional requirements and common issues specific to Fort Worth. This localized knowledge aids in tailoring estate plans that best protect your interests, ensuring that every critical detail aligns with the state's legal framework and local practices.

Engaging a local attorney means easier consultations and the opportunity for face-to-face meetings, helping foster a better understanding of your needs and goals. With our client-centric approach, Crain & Wooley prioritizes clear communication and transparency, offering guidance every step of the way. We emphasize understanding your long-term objectives and building robust estate plans that reflect your values and future goals. Collaborating with a Fort Worth attorney provides not only legal accuracy but also a partnership based on trust and tailored advice attuned to your individual situation.
Crain & Wooley is ready to help you. Get in touch via online form or call (972) 945-1610.
Commonly Asked Questions About Wills
What happens if I die without a will?

If you pass away without a valid will, your estate may be contested by other parties, leading to potential court battles for your intended beneficiaries. It is best to hire an experienced lawyer to draft your last will and testament to avoid such scenarios.
How Does a Will Affect Estate Taxes?

Having a well-drafted will can significantly affect how estate taxes are handled upon your passing. A comprehensive will outlines not only the distribution of assets but also helps to strategize around potential estate taxes. In Fort Worth, as part of Texas, there is no state-level estate tax, but federal estate taxes may apply if the estate's value exceeds the federal threshold. Effectively planning your estate with a legal professional ensures that you can utilize available tax credits and exemptions, thus potentially reducing the tax burden on your heirs.

Including tax planning strategies within a will can mean setting up trusts that minimize the taxable estate or allocating assets in a way that leverages marital deductions. An estate plan could include provisions for managing the impact of taxes on investment portfolios and other assets, thereby preserving more of the estate’s value for your beneficiaries. Consulting with a will attorney who knows both state and federal tax implications can save significant amounts, protecting and maximizing your estate’s longevity and financial contribution to your beneficiaries.
When should I update my will?

You should update your will when significant life events occur, such as marriage, divorce, birth of a child, or acquisition of new assets. It is important to review and make changes to your will to reflect your current circumstances.
What Are DIY Wills and Their Risks?

DIY wills have become increasingly common as people seek to assert control over their estate planning without incurring legal fees. However, DIY wills come with significant risks, particularly if they do not comply with state-specific legal requirements, which can render them invalid. In Fort Worth, a DIY will may overlook critical elements such as witness signatures or specific bequests, potentially leading to disputes or an unintended distribution of your assets.

While a DIY will may seem cost-effective initially, errors or omissions can lead to costly legal battles later on, far outweighing any upfront savings. Working with a qualified will lawyer ensures compliance with Texas laws and helps personalize the will according to your unique needs, minimizing the chance of future disputes among beneficiaries. This preventative approach could safeguard your interests and protect your loved ones from potential legal challenges.
Can I appoint a guardian for my minor children in my will?

Yes, you can designate a guardian for your minor children in your will. This allows you to specify who will take care of your children in the event of your passing, providing peace of mind for you and your family.
How Secure Is My Will Once Executed?

Once executed, the security of your will is paramount to ensure that your final wishes are honored. It is essential that the original will is stored in a safe, secure location where it can be easily accessed upon your passing, such as a safety deposit box or with your attorney. Many opt to store their will in safe deposit boxes or with their will attorney to prevent unauthorized access or potential loss or damage.

Discussing the storage location with trusted family members or your designated executor can prevent confusion and ensure that your will is readily available when needed. Knowing who is holding the original documentation can avoid unnecessary legal hurdles during the probate process. It is also wise to keep a non-binding copy in a secondary location as a backup while ensuring the placement of the original is clear and accessible to your executor or attorney.
Contact Us to Get Started Drafting Your Will

At Crain & Wooley, we understand that you want to feel confident knowing your valuable assets and property will be passed on to your loved ones, which is why we are here to help you draft a will that is tailored to your unique estate planning goals.

If you have additional questions regarding drafting a will, then please get in touch with a will attorney at our firm so you can meet our friendly team. When you choose our law firm, we will take the time to get to know you and become familiar with the specific details of your circumstances so that we can devise a customized legal strategy.

Protect Your Loved Ones with a Living Will or Advance Directive

Advance directives are legal documents that allow you to make important decisions about your medical care should you become unable to communicate your wishes. There are two types of advance directives: a living will and a durable power of attorney for healthcare. The former allows you to specify the medical procedures you would or would not like to receive in certain situations, while the latter appoints someone you trust to make medical decisions on your behalf.

Advance directives are crucial legal tools empowering individuals to retain control over their healthcare decisions even when incapacitated. They provide a clear roadmap for medical professionals and family members during critical times, avoiding conflicts or uncertainties. Crain & Wooley ensures these documents comply with Texas laws and are tailored to reflect your most current and comprehensive medical directives.
Do I Need An Advance Directive or a Living Will?

Advance directives are important for anyone who wants to take control of what happens to them when they are unable to speak for themselves. They also provide peace of mind for family members who may be unsure of what to do in a medical crisis. Our team of advance directive attorneys in Fort Worth can help you create an advance directive that reflects your wishes, whether you live in Dallas, Plano, or anywhere else in the greater Dallas area.
Call us at (972) 945-1610 to speak with a compassionate living will attorney near you.
What Are The Benefits of a Living Will?

A living will is a document that outlines your wishes regarding medical care in certain situations. It can be useful in various circumstances, including when you are terminally ill, in a coma, or otherwise unable to communicate your wishes. A living will can also help you avoid unwanted medical treatment and reduce the burden on your family members who may be unsure of what to do.

Having a living will is particularly essential in today’s evolving healthcare environment. It ensures that your ethical, religious, and personal preferences are honored even in situations where you cannot vocalize these preferences. Moreover, it serves as a testament to your foresight in easing the emotional and moral burden on your loved ones during challenging times. This document is continually kept up to date to reflect changes in your desires or advancements in medical technologies.

In a living will, you can specify the medical procedures you would or would not like to receive in certain situations. This includes situations such as when you are terminally ill, unconscious, or in a vegetative state. You can also state whether you would like to be taken off life support if it cannot be reversed. Additionally, you can choose to donate your organs or body to science. Your living will should be updated regularly to ensure that it reflects your current wishes.
Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare in Fort Worth

A durable power of attorney for healthcare allows you to appoint someone you trust to make important medical decisions on your behalf if you are unable to speak for yourself. It is especially important to have a durable power of attorney for healthcare if you have a serious medical condition, such as a terminal illness, or if you are elderly. If you do not have a durable power of attorney for healthcare, a judge may have to appoint a guardian to make medical decisions for you.

A durable power of attorney for healthcare allows you to choose the person you trust most to make important medical decisions on your behalf. This person is known as the agent. The agent can make medical decisions for you if you are unable to communicate your wishes, such as whether to take you off life support if it cannot be reversed or to donate your organs or body to science. The durable power of attorney for healthcare can also name a successor agent if the person you originally chose is unable to serve.
How To Choose An Agent

Choosing an agent through a durable power of attorney for healthcare is not just about naming someone; it involves selecting an individual who understands your values and is committed to respecting your wishes. It's critical to have candid discussions with your chosen agent about your medical preferences and values. This robust tool ensures that someone aligned with your views is legally empowered to make real-time critical decisions, offering you the peace of mind that every decision made aligns with your intentions.
How to Create an Advance Directive

Creating an advance directive is a simple process you can complete with the help of an experienced attorney. Our team at Crain & Wooley can help you create a living will or a durable power of attorney for healthcare that accurately reflects your wishes. We can also help you update your advance directive as your situation or wishes change.

The creation of an advance directive involves not just filling out forms but engaging in meaningful one-on-one consultations to ensure every detail is addressed. During these sessions, we delve deep into our clients’ concerns and specific needs, offering tailored advice and practical solutions to match their unique circumstances. We also provide updates on relevant legal changes that may impact your documents, underscoring our commitment to safeguarding your healthcare intentions over time.
Understanding Texas Advance Directive Laws

In Texas, the laws surrounding advance directives stipulate specific criteria that must be met for these documents to be legally binding. It's essential that these documents are signed by the principal and witnessed by two disinterested witnesses, neither of whom should be related by blood or marriage. This ensures the directives are carried out according to the individual’s authentic decisions, free of undue influence. Additionally, understanding the distinctions between a living will and a medical power of attorney is important for compliance and execution.

Texas law has specific guidelines for caregivers and medical facilities in handling advance directives. It’s recommended to store these documents where they can be easily accessed, especially during emergency medical situations. At Crain & Wooley, we advise clients on proper storage options and how to keep loved ones informed of their healthcare wishes. By staying informed of these local laws and requirements, clients gain peace of mind that their healthcare preferences will be respected and adhered to without intervention.
Contact a reliable advance directive lawyer in Fort Worth now. Dial (972) 945-1610 or use our online form to get started right away.
FAQ About Advance Directives or Living Wills in Fort Worth
What Is the Difference Between a Living Will and a Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare?

The primary difference between a living will and a durable power of attorney for healthcare lies in their functions. A living will documents your specific wishes regarding medical treatments under certain conditions. This is an independent legal document that instructs medical personnel on the treatments that align or do not align with your choices. Conversely, a durable power of attorney for healthcare appoints someone—referred to as your 'agent'—to make healthcare decisions for you if you are incapacitated. This person steps in to make decisions on scenarios not necessarily covered in your living will, ensuring all decisions reflect your personal values and desires.
Why Are Advance Directives Important in Medical Emergencies?

Advance directives hold paramount importance during medical emergencies as they communicate your healthcare preferences clearly when you cannot. They dictate the course of medical actions rather than leaving decisions to relatives who may be uninformed about your wishes. These documents help to ease potential disagreements among family members and provide a clear legal outline of your healthcare decisions to medical staff, ensuring treatment parallels your stated wishes. In Fort Worth, advance directives are respected as a crucial part of emergency healthcare planning, providing essential guidance for quick and difficult decision-making processes. For experienced guidance, schedule an appointment with a skilled advance directive attorney in Fort Worth at Crain & Wooley.
How Often Should You Update Your Advance Directive?

It is advisable to review and update your advance directive regularly, ensuring it aligns with your current values, health status, and treatment preferences. Life events such as marriage, divorce, the diagnosis of a significant medical condition, or milestone birthdays are opportune times to revisit your directives. Owning an outdated directive may lead to doubts among medical personnel and family members about your real intentions. At Crain & Wooley, we recommend setting periodic reminders—perhaps every five years or after any major personal change—to reassess and possibly update your healthcare documents.
Can My Advance Directive Be Changed After It’s Created?

Yes, you can modify, update, or revoke your advance directive at any time, provided you are still competent to make decisions. Changes should be documented in writing, signed, and dated. Once updated, it’s essential to inform relevant parties, including your healthcare providers, family members, and your chosen healthcare agent, to prevent misinformation during emergencies. By keeping everyone informed, your intentions are preserved, and healthcare actions remain consistent with your wishes. Our team can assist you in making these adjustments, ensuring everything is legally sound and communicated.
Who Should Have a Copy of My Advance Directive?

It's critical to ensure that multiple parties have copies of your advance directive. Key individuals include your assigned healthcare agent, primary care physician, attorney, and family members. Keeping copies with these people ensures that in any critical medical situation, your preferences are immediately accessible and adhered to. Crain & Wooley also recommends discussing the contents of your directive with each copy holder, so there is no ambiguity about your healthcare decisions, thereby honoring your healthcare preferences efficiently during crucial times.
Call us at (972) 945-1610 to speak with a compassionate living will attorney near you.

Protecting What You Leave For Your Children

When you think about your child’s future, you probably picture school events, first jobs, and milestones, not court-supervised accounts or a teenager suddenly receiving a large inheritance. Planning ahead with a clear minor trust can help you protect what you leave and guide how it supports your child over time.

At Crain & Wooley , we help families in the Dallas–Fort Worth area create plans that keep assets available for a child’s needs while avoiding unnecessary court involvement and confusion. Our team focuses on making complex concepts understandable so you can make decisions with confidence.

We offer proactive planning at clear, flat-rate fees, and we meet with clients in person or virtually, including by appointment in Fort Worth. Our goal is to give you clarity about your options, answer your questions directly, and build a plan that reflects your values and your family.

Planning for your child’s future starts with the right structure. Call (972) 945-1610 or contact our team to speak with a Fort Worth minor trust attorney about creating a plan that protects your child’s inheritance.
Why Families Use Minor Trusts

Many parents and grandparents are surprised to learn that leaving money directly to a child can lead to court oversight that may not match their wishes. If a minor receives assets outright under a will or as a named beneficiary, a Texas court may become involved to manage those funds.

That kind of court process can limit flexibility and may not provide the thoughtful guidance you want for your child. A minor trust allows you to choose a responsible adult to manage funds, define what those funds can be used for, and decide when and how your child will receive money as they grow.

Instead of a young adult suddenly inheriting everything at eighteen, you can direct that assets help with education, health care, and reasonable living expenses, then transition to more access as your child matures. A minor trust lawyer Fort Worth can explain how these choices work under Texas law so you know what fits your situation.

This kind of planning is especially important when there are life insurance policies, retirement accounts, or other significant assets intended for children. Without a trust, those funds may be paid directly to a guardian or held for the child in ways that do not reflect your personal priorities.
How Our Firm Helps Fort Worth Parents

When you work with us, we start by listening. We ask about your children, your goals, and any concerns you have about family dynamics, financial responsibility, or future education plans. Parents in Fort Worth often tell us they want to protect their children without creating tension among relatives or making the process feel intimidating.

We provide minor trust planning as part of a comprehensive estate plan at clear, upfront flat rates. You know the cost of proactive planning before you commit, which helps you budget and prevents surprise invoices later. If a situation arises that would require separate hourly work, such as contested probate, we explain that clearly in advance.

Our team believes that education should come first. During your meetings, we explain options in everyday language, not legal jargon, and we encourage questions until you feel comfortable with each decision. We also share resources such as seminars and guides so you can revisit information at your own pace.

Responsiveness is a core part of our service. We commit to returning messages about important matters promptly, and we put that commitment in writing with a money-back guarantee related to our responsiveness. For many parents, simply knowing they will not be left waiting for answers is a major source of relief.

Because families and laws change, we also offer a lifetime guarantee option that includes review and updating of your documents over time. This can be especially valuable for minor trusts because your child’s needs at age five will look very different from their needs at age twenty-five.

We serve the Dallas–Fort Worth region from offices in Plano and Mansfield, and we meet with clients in Fort Worth by appointment. If it is easier for your family, we can conduct meetings virtually so you can move your planning forward without long drives or complicated schedules.
Steps To Create A Minor Trust

Once you decide to explore a minor trust, the process does not have to be overwhelming. We follow a structured path so you always know what comes next and what we need from you at each stage. Our aim is to keep decisions organized and manageable.
Here is how the process typically works:

items: Initial consultation: We discuss your family, assets, and goals, and we explain how a minor trust fits with your broader estate plan under Texas law.
Information gathering: You provide details about accounts, life insurance, and any existing documents such as wills or beneficiary designations.
Design decisions: Together we decide who will serve as trustee and backup trustee, how funds may be used, and at what ages or milestones your child can receive funds directly.
Document drafting: Our team prepares your trust and related documents based on the choices you make, using clear language that matches your instructions.
Review meeting: We walk through each document with you, explain key sections, and answer questions so you understand what you are signing.
Signing and implementation: You sign documents following Texas formalities, and we guide you on aligning beneficiary designations and accounts with the new trust.
Ongoing questions: If concerns come up later, you can contact us, and our responsiveness commitment helps ensure you receive timely answers.

bodyBlocks.text: Throughout this process, a minor trust attorney Fort Worth from our team works to keep you informed and comfortable. We also clarify which services are covered by your flat-rate plan and when any additional work might be billed separately, so you always know where you stand.
Key Decisions For Your Child’s Future

Creating a minor trust involves more than filling in blanks on a form. You are making thoughtful choices about who will guide your child’s inheritance and how that support should look over many years. We help you work through these choices in a practical, respectful way.

One of the most important decisions is who will serve as trustee. Parents in the Fort Worth area often consider siblings, close friends, or other trusted adults. We talk through factors such as financial responsibility, values, health, and whether the person is likely to be available when the trust is needed.

Distance can be another factor. Some families prefer a trustee who lives close to the child, while others select someone outside Fort Worth due to family relationships. We discuss how location may affect communication and administration without telling you that there is only one right answer.

You also decide how and when your child will receive funds. Some parents direct that the trustee may use money for education, healthcare, and reasonable living costs, then release funds in stages at certain ages. Others prefer broader trustee discretion with guidance about values and priorities.

Families with more complex situations, such as blended families, children from prior relationships, or a child with a disability, often need additional coordination. We help you consider how a minor trust can work with other planning tools so that each child is treated fairly according to your wishes.

A minor trust lawyer Fort Worth on our team will walk you through examples and common patterns, then help you adapt those ideas to your specific family. The goal is a plan that feels both realistic and aligned with your hopes for your child’s future.
Keeping Your Minor Trust Up To Date

Your child’s life will not stay the same, and your estate plan should have room to adapt. The trust provisions that made sense when your child was in elementary school might not fit as well when they are approaching college or starting a career.

Significant life events, such as the birth of another child, remarriage, changes in financial circumstances, or shifts in Texas law, can all be good reasons to review your minor trust. We work to make that review process straightforward rather than something you feel you need to avoid.

Our lifetime guarantee option is designed for families who want ongoing support in keeping documents current. With that option, your plan can be periodically reviewed and updated so that key details like trustees, distribution ages, and instructions stay aligned with your real life.

We also provide educational resources to help you recognize when a change might be appropriate. When questions arise, our responsiveness commitment means you can reach out to our team and receive guidance instead of feeling stuck or uncertain.

For parents and grandparents in this area, knowing that their plan can grow along with their children often brings a sense of long-term security. You are not locked into today’s choices forever, and you do not have to navigate changes alone.
Frequently Asked Questions
How much does it cost to set up a minor trust?

We offer minor trust planning as part of flat-rate estate planning packages. Your fee depends on the scope of your overall plan, not on hourly billing. During your initial consultation, we explain the total cost before you decide to move forward, so there are no surprise legal bills.
Do I really need a minor trust if I already have a will?

A will is essential, but it typically leaves assets outright unless it includes trust provisions. If you leave funds directly to a minor, a court may need to get involved. We review your existing documents and explain whether a minor trust would add protection for your child.
How do I choose the right trustee for my child?

The best trustee is usually someone you trust to be organized, fair, and able to handle money wisely. We help you think through options, including potential conflicts and backup choices, and we discuss how different people might handle the role so you can make an informed decision.
Can we meet virtually if we live in Fort Worth?

Yes, many Fort Worth families choose virtual meetings for convenience. We also offer by-appointment meetings in Fort Worth and have offices in Plano and Mansfield. You can pick the format that works best for your schedule while still receiving the same level of guidance and communication.
What happens if laws or my family situation change later?

When laws or family circumstances change, it is often wise to review your minor trust. With our lifetime guarantee option, we can update documents so they stay effective. Even without that option, you can consult us about revisions, and we will explain what updates may be appropriate.
Talk With Our Team Today

Creating a minor trust is one of the most practical ways to care for your child’s future. Instead of leaving their inheritance to chance, you can choose who will manage it, how it can be used, and when your child will gain control, all within a clear Texas-based plan.

At Crain & Wooley, we work with families in the Fort Worth area and across Dallas–Fort Worth to make this planning understandable and manageable. With flat-rate pricing, education-focused meetings, and options for ongoing updates, we strive to remove as much stress as possible from the process.

Whether you prefer to meet virtually, at one of our offices, or in Fort Worth by appointment, we are ready to walk through your options together. A brief conversation can help you decide whether a minor trust is right for your family and what your next step should be.

If you want to make sure your child’s inheritance is managed wisely and distributed at the right time, a minor trust may help. Call (972) 945-1610 or contact our office to talk through your options with our Fort Worth estate planning team.

Guidance When Probate Turns Into Conflict

Losing a loved one is difficult enough. When questions arise about a will, a trust, or how the estate is being handled, the stress can quickly escalate. If you are facing a contested probate matter connected to Garland and you are not sure what to do next, Crain & Wooley is here to help.

We focus our work on estate planning and related areas such as probate, long term care planning, Medicaid planning, and tax strategies. That focus means we spend our days thinking about how plans are created and how they play out in real families, including when disputes arise. Our offices in the Dallas–Fort Worth region allow us to work closely with families whose probate cases are handled in the courts that serve Garland.

We approach every situation with clarity, confidence, and compassion. Our team walks you through your options in plain language, explains what the probate courts generally require, and helps you decide how to move forward. If you need a probate litigation attorney Garland families can turn to for grounded advice, we are ready to listen.
Families facing probate disputes often want reassurance that they are working with a trusted legal team. You can learn more about our client experiences by reading our clients' reviews.

If you believe a will, trust, or estate is being mishandled, speaking with a probate litigation attorney can help you understand your rights. Call (972) 945-1610 or contact our office to discuss your probate dispute with a Garland probate litigation lawyer.
When Probate Conflicts Arise

Many families assume that once someone passes away, the will is read and the estate is divided without difficulty. In reality, disagreements are common, especially when there were last minute changes, blended families, or unclear promises. If you live in or have a connection to Garland, your matter will typically move through the Dallas County probate system, which follows specific procedures and deadlines.

Contested situations often arise when someone believes the will does not reflect the person’s true wishes, when a trust is being interpreted in a way that feels unfair, or when an executor is not communicating. Disputes can also result from long term use of powers of attorney, especially if one family member handled finances before death and others now question those decisions. These conflicts rarely stay purely legal, because they rest on years of family history.

Left unaddressed, a probate dispute can lead to missed filing deadlines, ongoing expense, and deepening divides within the family. Executors and trustees also face personal risk if they fail to carry out their fiduciary duties correctly. We work with heirs, beneficiaries, executors, and surviving spouses to help them understand their rights and responsibilities so they can make informed choices before positions harden.

Our goal is to help you see how your specific facts fit within the broader rules that govern probate matters in the courts serving Garland. Once you see the legal framework, it becomes easier to separate emotion from strategy and to decide when negotiation may resolve things and when a more formal challenge may be necessary. A probate litigation lawyer from our team can help you evaluate those options step by step.
How Our Probate Litigation Lawyers Help

When you contact us about a probate dispute, our first priority is to understand your story. From there, a probate litigation lawyer Garland families can work with will walk you through the possible legal paths.

We typically begin with an initial assessment in which we discuss potential grounds for a will contest or other claim, such as lack of capacity, undue influence, improper execution, or breach of fiduciary duty. We also look at defenses that may apply if you are in the position of defending a will or an executor’s actions. This assessment is designed to help you weigh the strength of your position and the practical implications of different choices.

Once we agree on an approach, we help you gather information that may be needed for negotiations or court filings. That may include medical records, financial records, correspondence, or prior versions of planning documents. Throughout this phase, we focus on client education. We explain why certain information matters, how it may be used, and what steps typically follow in the courts that handle probate for Garland estates.

Many probate disputes can be addressed through communication, negotiation, or mediation. We work to identify whether a negotiated resolution is realistic and in your interest. When litigation is required, we guide you through each stage, such as pleadings, discovery, hearings, and potential trial. We also talk with you about timing, because the pace of a case can be influenced by the court’s docket, the number of parties involved, and the complexity of the estate.

Communication is central to how we practice. Our firm has a clear commitment to responsive communication, backed by a money back guarantee tied to responsiveness on important matters. You will know how to reach us, when to expect responses, and what the next step in your case is likely to be. We want you to feel informed rather than left in the dark while a probate litigation attorney works with you.

Because our primary work is estate planning and probate related fields, we bring a planner’s eye to contested cases. We understand how wills and trusts are intended to function and how powers of attorney interact with later probate. That perspective can uncover alternatives, such as clarifying language, revising related planning for surviving family, or resolving disputes in a way that protects long term goals rather than only winning a narrow point.
Probate disputes often involve complex family and financial issues. You can review examples of how our firm has helped clients resolve difficult estate matters by exploring our case results.

Cost is one of the first questions most people have when they consider hiring a probate litigation attorney. We talk with you about your priorities and budget so we can tailor our approach. Our transparent service model is designed to minimize surprises. We draw a clear line between the planning work that is covered by flat rates and the reactive or litigation work that may need different billing arrangements. We revisit these conversations when major strategic decisions arise so that cost never feels like a hidden variable. This is especially important when a contested matter involves property in or around Garland, because real estate and business interests can add layers of complexity.

We also set expectations around communication. Our team supports in person meetings at our Plano and Mansfield offices, by appointment service in Fort Worth, and virtual consultations for those who live out of the area. Many heirs or beneficiaries connected to a Garland estate do not live locally, so we use video conferences, secure document sharing, and phone calls to keep everyone aligned.

We strive to respond promptly to your calls and emails. Our responsiveness money back guarantee reflects how seriously we take this commitment. While we cannot control every external factor in a probate dispute, we can control how clearly and quickly we communicate with you. Our aim is that you never wonder what is happening with your case or what you should be doing next.

Expanding Your Knowledge: FAQs on Estate Planning
What Are the Benefits of Having a Power of Attorney?

A power of attorney (POA) is a crucial legal tool that grants an individual the authority to make decisions on another's behalf, particularly useful in situations where seniors are unable to manage their affairs due to illness or incapacitation. There are several types of POAs, including general, durable, and medical, each serving specific needs. Establishing a POA ensures that someone trusted is designated to handle financial and healthcare decisions, which can be critical in maintaining stability and security. Additionally, a POA can help prevent court proceedings for guardianship, saving time, stress, and expense. Understanding the differences and implementing the correct type of POA with Crain & Wooley’s guidance allows seniors and their families to be better prepared for future events, ensuring peace of mind.
Why Is It Important to Regularly Review Estate Plans?

Regularly reviewing your estate plan is crucial, especially as laws and family circumstances evolve. Changes in tax laws, state regulations, or personal situations such as marriage, divorce, or the birth of children necessitate updates to ensure plans remain relevant and effective. Missteps in maintaining updated documents can lead to unintended beneficiaries or outdated healthcare provisions, which might not reflect one's current wishes. Consistent reviews allow for the inclusion of new financial information, such as changes in income or assets, that impact how resources should be managed. Collaborating with Crain & Wooley ensures that your estate plan is frequently assessed and aligned with both personal goals and legal standards, ultimately protecting your legacy.

Get Skillful Guidance Through the Probate Process

Probate is a notoriously complex area of the law that has the potential to frustrate, confuse, and overwhelm. If you have been named Executor of a will, you need a seasoned lawyer on your side to help you carry out your duties efficiently and properly. In Mansfield, the law firm you need is Crain & Wooley. Our Mansfield probate lawyer focuses solely on estate planning and probate matters, ensuring that you can feel confident in our qualifications and the quality of our representation. We provide both the legal knowledge and compassionate support necessary to navigate this challenging time, ensuring your loved one's wishes are honored with dignity and precision.
Crain & Wooley is ready to represent your best interests. Contact us by calling (972) 945-1610 now to connect with an experienced Mansfield probate attorney.
What Is Probate?

Probate is the legal process where estate assets and property previously owned by a deceased person are analyzed and transferred to their loved ones. The court oversees the payment of outstanding debts by the decedent (the deceased person) and the distribution of their assets—this process is also known as probate administration. Understanding the probate process is crucial for executors and beneficiaries alike, as it dictates how the decedent's wishes are honored. Additionally, probate can involve interpreting the will, identifying and gathering the decedent’s assets, and addressing any disputes that might arise among beneficiaries. It is important to note that the necessity and complexity of probate varies, which is why consulting with knowledgeable legal professionals like those at Crain & Wooley can make a significant difference in managing the process smoothly.
Types of Probate in Texas
In Texas, there are several types of probate proceedings depending on the type of estate that you have:

items: Formal Probate: Required if the estate is valued at more than $75,000, excluding specific types of exempt property. This process involves court supervision and is the most common form of probate. It includes court hearings and sometimes can be lengthy, particularly if any disputes arise.

Muniment of Title: A simplified process that allows for the transfer of assets if there is a valid will. This method is faster and does not require the full probate process if the will is uncontested, making it an appealing option if the estate is straightforward.

Small Estate Affidavits: This may be applicable in certain circumstances for people who inherit property. A simple affidavit can be prepared to collect property without going through formal probate, though it requires meeting specific legal criteria under Texas law.

Small Estate Procedures: If the total value of the estate does not exceed the amount needed to pay the surviving spouse, minor children, any adult incapacitated children, and certain creditors, this may be applicable, offering a less complicated route. This option reduces the administrative burden on the estate and is intended to support families with fewer assets.

bodyBlocks.text: Speak with a seasoned Mansfield probate lawyer at Crain & Wooley. Contact us online or call (972) 945-1610.
Benefits of Hiring a Probate Lawyer in Mansfield

Navigating the probate process can be particularly challenging, especially when grieving a loss. Obtaining the services of a qualified probate lawyer can provide the guidance and support needed to ensure a seamless and effective probate procedure. Here are some benefits of hiring a probate lawyer:

items: Expert Legal Guidance: A probate lawyer possesses sufficient knowledge to manage each phase of the probate process, including document submission, resolution of disputes, and asset distribution.

Legal Protection: By ensuring that the probate process is conducted properly and in compliance with the law, an attorney can assist in defending your rights and interests. They will help you avoid legal pitfalls that can arise during the process.

Efficiency: Hiring an attorney can save you time and prevent expensive errors that could cause delays in transferring assets. Their experience streamlines the process, making it less burdensome. Additionally, having an attorney manage notifications to creditors and court filings reduces stress and minimizes the risk of potential delays or errors.

Peace of Mind: Knowing that your probate matter is being handled by a knowledgeable professional can provide reassurance during a difficult time, allowing you to focus on healing rather than legal complexities. This peace of mind is invaluable, especially when it involves preserving family relationships and ensuring the decedent's wishes are fulfilled with care.

bodyBlocks.text: Don't hesitate to schedule a consultation if you are going through probate. With empathy and knowledge, our probate lawyers in Mansfield at Crain & Wooley are here to support you. Get in touch as soon as possible.
Probate FAQ

Death and taxes are great equalizers. Each of us, no matter age, race, gender, or socio-economic status, encounters death and pays taxes. But who knew dying could be so complicated? There are many misconceptions regarding the steps needed to secure a prosperous future for yourself and your heirs. One widespread misconception is that having a last will and testament avoids the probate process, yet the court system is full of will-based probate cases. How is this possible?
Let’s explore a few common questions surrounding the creation and implementation of a last will and testament.
What is a will?

A will is typically a typewritten document that must be witnessed by at least two disinterested individuals and signed in front of a Notary Public. It contains information explaining what you want to happen to your assets after you are no longer here.
Does a last will and testament allow my heirs to avoid probate proceedings?

No. Probate is required if your estate is worth more than $75,000. Before your heirs can inherit assets left to them, an application for probate must be filed in the probate court in the county where you passed away. Usually, a probate application must be filed within four years of your passing.
What are the steps to probate a will in Texas?
Fulfilling your last wishes set forth in your will requires your executor to complete an eight-step process:

items: Filing with the court: Submit the will and a petition for probate.
Posting notice: Notify interested parties of the probate proceedings.
Validating the will: Ensure that the will is valid and meets Texas requirements.
Inventory of assets: List and appraise the decedent's assets.
Identifying beneficiaries: Determine who will inherit according to the will.
Notifying creditors: Inform creditors of the probate proceedings and settle outstanding debts.
Resolution of disputes: Address any disputes that arise regarding the will or estate.
Distribution of assets: Distribute the remaining assets to the beneficiaries.

bodyBlocks.text: This process can be quite lengthy and costly, taking anywhere from six months to multiple years depending on the complexity of your estate.
How much does the probate process cost?

Each probate situation differs in total costs incurred, but the types of costs sustained are generally the same across the board. Costs may include court fees, attorney fees, accounting fees, appraisal fees, and other miscellaneous charges that must all be paid prior to the distribution of assets.
Are all assets subject to probate?
Not all assets are probated in Texas and may pass outside of the will. Assets that are not subject to probate include, but are not limited to, the following:

items: Insurance policies
401(k) plans
Pensions
Funds held in trust
Joint Tenants with Right of Survivorship
Payable on Death bank accounts

bodyBlocks.text: Does my estate have to hire an attorney to go through the probate process?

Most of the time, yes. Texas courts usually require an executor to be represented by an attorney in a probate matter because an executor represents both themselves and the interests of beneficiaries and creditors.
What options are available to avoid probate proceedings?
Avoiding probate for your estate in Texas can save your heirs time, money, and the hassle of going through the court process. Here are some effective strategies:

items: Revocable Living Trust: You can manage and retain control over your assets during your lifetime by putting them in a revocable living trust. The assets pass immediately to your beneficiaries upon your death, bypassing the probate process.
Beneficiary Designations: If you name beneficiaries, assets such as retirement plans, life insurance policies, and payable-on-death (POD) or transfer-on-death (TOD) accounts may avoid probate.
Joint Ownership: When you own property jointly with rights of survivorship, ownership transfers to the remaining owner automatically and avoids probate upon your passing.

Transfer on Death Deeds: If you own real estate, you can complete a Transfer on Death Deed to choose a beneficiary who will inherit the property without going through the probate process upon your passing.
Gifting: You can donate assets to lower the amount of your estate and potentially prevent those assets from going through probate.
Small Estate Affidavit: Your heirs may be allowed to bypass formal probate if your estate meets certain requirements under Texas law and is sufficiently small.

bodyBlocks.text: Consulting with an experienced estate planning attorney can help you choose the best strategies to meet your needs and ensure your estate plan is properly executed.
What is a revocable living trust?

A revocable living trust directs the administration of assets during your life and specifies how your estate will be distributed at your death, allowing trust beneficiaries to receive assets without court interference.
What Rights Do Beneficiaries Have in Probate in Texas?

Beneficiaries play a pivotal role in the probate process and have rights that protect their interests. They are entitled to information about the estate and its administration status, ensuring they can see the process's transparency. Beneficiaries possess the right to receive their designated share of the estate according to what’s stipulated in the will. Furthermore, they hold the right to contest the will if they suspect it doesn’t reflect the true intentions of the decedent or if there are concerns about the will’s validity, such as undue influence or lack of capacity.

In Texas, beneficiaries are empowered to request a formal accounting of the estate from the executor, demanding clarity on how distributions are handled. Failing to respond adequately to these rights by executors can lead beneficiaries to seek legal action, emphasizing the executor’s responsibility to act diligently and in good faith. Understanding these rights is critical for beneficiaries to ensure they receive what is rightfully theirs and to address any grievances satisfactorily within the bounds of the law.
How Long Does the Probate Process Typically Take in Mansfield?

The duration of the probate process in Mansfield, as is typical in other parts of Texas, can vary widely based on several factors. Typically, an uncomplicated probate proceeding might conclude within six months to a year. However, if the estate is complex, involving multiple assets or disputes among beneficiaries, the process could extend to several years. Lengthy disputes surrounding the will's validity or valuation of rare assets are common contributors to delays.

Other factors influencing the timeline include the workload of the Tarrant County Probate Court and the responsiveness and efficiency of the executor and beneficiaries in fulfilling their responsibilities. Having well-organized documentation and clear communication can significantly expedite proceedings. Engaging with a knowledgeable probate lawyer in Mansfield is an effective way to navigate these timelines, as they can foresee potential delays and manage them proactively, providing clarity and relief from the uncertainties associated with probate duration.
What Happens if Someone Dies Without a Will in Texas?

When an individual passes away without a will, their estate is considered “intestate,” and Texas laws take over regarding distribution. Typically, the estate is divided according to the state's intestacy statutes, which prioritize spouses and children for inheritance. The probate court in Mansfield will supervise the appointment of an administrator. This person performs many roles similar to an executor, such as paying debts and distributing assets as specified under state law.

If no immediate family member like a spouse or child exists, the estate may be distributed to extended relatives. Without careful consideration and planning, dying intestate could mean assets do not pass in accordance with what the deceased might have verbally expressed, or to distant relatives previously uninvolved in the decedent's life. Engaging with probate lawyers like those at Crain & Wooley can help families understand this process and ensure administrative roles proceed smoothly, conforming to Texas law.
